content.text: Claude autonomously navigates web pages and fills form inputs
by inspecting the rendered DOM, discovering elements, and executing interactions without you writing test code manually.

attrs.alt: Claude-driven form filling and navigation concept diagram

content.text:
Claude uses Playwright (via MCP or CLI) to:
1.
: open page → snapshot → interact using refs → snapshot again when DOM changes.
Claude discovers elements via accessibility tree (roles, text, refs)
Describe what to test in plain English; Claude executes
that persist across DOM changes until you navigate.
Based on the Playwright Form Automator skill:

---
description: Manually test a site and create a report
---

### Instructions
1. Use Playwright CLI/MCP to test the scenario
2. Navigate to the URL and perform interactions
3. Observe and verify expected behavior
4. Use `playwright-cli screenshot` for evidence
5. Report findings in natural language:
   - Steps performed
   - Outcomes observed
   - Issues or accessibility concerns

/pw-manual-testing

Test the signup form on https://example.com/signup:
1. Fill email, password, name fields
2. Click "Continue"
3. Verify navigation to dashboard
Claude executes all steps and generates a detailed MD report with screenshots.
For complex flows requiring multiple pages:

# Step 1: Navigate to login
page.goto('https://app.com/login')
page.screenshot(path='/tmp/login.png')

# Step 2: Fill credentials
page.locator('#username').fill('user')
page.locator('#password').fill('pass')
page.locator('button[type="submit"]').click()

# Step 3: Wait for dashboard
page.wait_for_selector('#dashboard')
page.screenshot(path='/tmp/dashboard.png')

# Step 4: Navigate to settings
page.locator('role=link[text="Settings"]').click()
page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')

# Step 5: Update profile
page.locator('#name').fill('New Name')
page.locator('#bio').fill('Updated bio')
page.locator('role=button[text="Save"]').click()
Fills text, dates, file uploads automatically
- Pauses for manual approval before final submit
- View API calls during form submission
Ensures dynamic content loads before interacting
DOM changes invalidate old refs; get fresh snapshot
Visual proof of what Claude saw/did
Create reusable functions for high-level actions
Loads large accessibility trees into context
Tabs, network, tracing, video, state management
Claude automatically discovers available commands through the installed skill.
: Open → snapshot → interact with refs (e12, e15) → snapshot again
: Describe what to test; Claude executes without manual test scripting
: Claude inspects accessibility tree for roles, text, test IDs instead of guessing CSS paths
: Form automator pauses for manual approval before final submission
: Manual findings easily become automated Playwright tests
: Playwright CLI replaced MCP for better token efficiency and agent integration
Claude gets "eyes" to see and interact with web applications like a human tester, but with consistency and reporting capabilities manual testing usually lacks.
When working with claude-driven form filling and navigation, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with claude-driven form filling and navigation. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of claude-driven form filling and navigation scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
